content.text: Sandbox and Isolated Test Environments
Shared dev and staging environments are convenient but fragile: two engineers testing simultaneously can corrupt each other's state, and a broken deploy to staging can block the entire team's CI.
Per-PR sandbox environments
solve this by giving each pull request its own isolated Kubernetes namespace, its own API key, and its own ephemeral database — created automatically when the PR opens and torn down when it closes.
A sandbox environment is not a replacement for staging — it is a safety layer that prevents one engineer's broken branch from blocking everyone else's integration testing.

This sub-subtopic covers the topology of sandbox environments for Claude applications, how to provision and tear them down automatically in CI, how to manage API key and rate-limit isolation, and when to use a sandbox versus the shared staging environment.

attrs.alt: Sandbox and isolated test environment topology for Claude apps

content.text: When Sandboxes Are Needed
Not every project needs per-PR sandboxes. They add infrastructure cost and complexity. The trigger for introducing them is shared-environment contention:
Symptom
Cause
Solution
CI fails intermittently because a dev deployed a broken build to dev
Shared dev namespace; any push overwrites
Per-PR sandbox isolates each branch
Staging is locked while a long red-team exercise runs
Staging is a single environment; one job occupies it
Dedicated red-team sandbox separate from CI staging
Two PRs conflict in dev because they share config state
Shared ConfigMap mutated by different tests
Per-PR namespace has its own ConfigMap
Rate limit errors in dev because multiple test suites run simultaneously
Single API key shared across all dev tests
Per-PR sandbox uses a dedicated low-tier key
Provisioning a Sandbox in GitHub Actions

The most practical implementation creates a Kubernetes namespace named after the PR number. All resources in the sandbox use this namespace, so they are fully isolated from other PRs and from shared environments.

# .github/workflows/sandbox.yml
name: PR Sandbox
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened, closed]

jobs:
  create-sandbox:
    if: github.event.action != 'closed'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set sandbox namespace
        run: echo "NAMESPACE=pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV

      - name: Create namespace
        env:
          KUBECONFIG: ${{ secrets.DEV_KUBECONFIG }}
        run: |
          kubectl create namespace $NAMESPACE --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

      - name: Deploy sandbox
        env:
          KUBECONFIG: ${{ secrets.DEV_KUBECONFIG }}
        run: |
          IMAGE=myregistry.io/claude-app:${{ github.sha }}
          kubectl set image deployment/claude-app app=$IMAGE -n $NAMESPACE ||           kubectl create deployment claude-app             --image=$IMAGE             --replicas=1             -n $NAMESPACE
          kubectl rollout status deployment/claude-app -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=120s

      - name: Inject sandbox config
        env:
          KUBECONFIG: ${{ secrets.DEV_KUBECONFIG }}
        run: |
          kubectl create secret generic claude-api-key             --from-literal=ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY_SANDBOX }}             -n $NAMESPACE --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
          kubectl create configmap claude-app-config             --from-literal=APP_ENV=sandbox             --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG             -n $NAMESPACE --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

      - name: Post sandbox URL to PR
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
        run: |
          SANDBOX_URL="https://pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}.sandbox.acme.internal"
          gh pr comment ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}             --body "Sandbox deployed: $SANDBOX_URL (namespace: $NAMESPACE)"

  destroy-sandbox:
    if: github.event.action == 'closed'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Delete namespace on PR close
        env:
          KUBECONFIG: ${{ secrets.DEV_KUBECONFIG }}
        run: |
          NAMESPACE=pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          kubectl delete namespace $NAMESPACE --ignore-not-found
          echo "Sandbox $NAMESPACE destroyed"
API Key Isolation in Sandboxes

Each sandbox must use its own API key — or at minimum, a shared sandbox-pool key separate from dev, staging, and production keys. Without isolation, concurrent sandbox tests from multiple PRs can collectively saturate the rate limit and cause spurious test failures.
Option 1: Pool of sandbox keys
Maintain a pool of 3–5 sandbox API keys and assign one to each active PR. Return the key to the pool when the PR closes:
Option 2: Low-rate shared sandbox key

For smaller teams, a single sandbox key with a deliberately low rate limit is sufficient. Sandboxes intentionally run fewer concurrent requests than staging, so a shared low-tier key rarely causes contention.
Sandbox Lifecycle Management
Sandboxes must be automatically torn down to prevent namespace sprawl. Two triggers:
PR closed (merged or abandoned):
The destroy-sandbox job in the workflow above handles this. The namespace and all its resources are deleted within minutes of the PR closing.
Timeout (safety net):
A stale sandbox from a PR closed without triggering the workflow consumes API quota and cluster resources indefinitely. Add a nightly sweep:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/sweep_stale_sandboxes.sh
# Delete sandbox namespaces for PRs that are no longer open

set -euo pipefail

for ns in $(kubectl get namespaces -o name | grep "namespace/pr-" | sed "s|namespace/||"); do
  pr_number="${ns#pr-}"
  # Check if the PR is still open via GitHub API
  state=$(gh pr view "$pr_number" --json state --jq .state 2>/dev/null || echo "NOTFOUND")
  if [ "$state" != "OPEN" ]; then
    echo "Deleting stale sandbox $ns (PR #$pr_number state: $state)"
    kubectl delete namespace "$ns" --ignore-not-found
  fi
done
Running Evals in a Sandbox
Sandbox environments support a targeted subset of evals — not the full regression suite. The goal is fast feedback on the specific change in the PR:

# .github/workflows/sandbox.yml (add after deploy)
  sandbox-smoke:
    needs: [create-sandbox]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install anthropic pytest

      - name: Run smoke evals against sandbox
        env:
          APP_URL: "https://pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}.sandbox.acme.internal"
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY_SANDBOX }}
        run: |
          pytest tests/evals/smoke/             --app-url=$APP_URL             --eval-threshold=0.85             --tb=short
Sandbox vs. Staging: When to Use Each
Use case
Use sandbox
Use staging
Validate a PR before review
Yes — isolated, fast
No — affects shared environment
Full regression eval suite
No — too expensive per PR
Yes — run here before prod gate
Security probe replay
No — only CI does this
Yes — part of Gate 3
Exploratory testing by QA
Yes — own namespace
Acceptable if no active CI run
Load testing
No — sandbox has 1 replica
Yes — staging has 2 replicas + prod-tier key
Red-team manual campaign
No — needs stable environment
Yes — or dedicated red-team env
Cost Management
Per-PR sandboxes run real API calls. Without cost controls, a busy team with 20 open PRs can generate significant API spend on sandbox smoke tests alone:
Limit smoke suite size:
Sandbox smoke tests should run ≤ 20 eval cases. Reserve the full 200-case suite for the staging gate.
Use haiku-class models:
Sandboxes do not need the same model as production for smoke tests — a smaller, cheaper model can validate routing and parsing logic. Only staging and production must match exactly.
Rate-limit sandbox API key:
Set the sandbox key's rate limit low (e.g. 5 requests/min). This prevents runaway tests and makes sandbox cost predictable.
Auto-pause idle sandboxes:
Scale the deployment to 0 replicas after 30 minutes of no API traffic; scale back up on the next request.
Sandbox Environment Configuration Reference
A Helm values file per environment is the cleanest way to manage the per-namespace configuration. Each sandbox gets the same chart as dev/staging/prod, with different values:

# helm/values-sandbox.yaml
replicaCount: 1
autoscaling:
  enabled: false

image:
  repository: myregistry.io/claude-app
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent
  # tag set at deploy time: --set image.tag=${{ github.sha }}

env:
  APP_ENV: sandbox
  LOG_LEVEL: DEBUG
  MAX_TOKENS: "512"         # reduced to limit sandbox API cost

resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 128Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi

# No ingress in sandbox — access via port-forward or cluster-internal URL
ingress:
  enabled: false

# Scale to 0 after 30 min idle (requires KEDA or a custom controller)
idleTimeout: 1800

# Deploy sandbox using Helm
helm upgrade --install   "claude-app-pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"   ./helm/claude-app   --namespace "pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"   --create-namespace   --values helm/values-sandbox.yaml   --set image.tag=${{ github.sha }}   --set envSecrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY_SANDBOX }}   --wait --timeout 120s

# Destroy
helm uninstall "claude-app-pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"   --namespace "pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
kubectl delete namespace "pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}" --ignore-not-found
Debugging in a Sandbox
Sandboxes are the right place to run exploratory debugging that would be disruptive in a shared environment. Common debugging workflows:

# Port-forward to the sandbox pod for direct access
kubectl port-forward   -n pr-42   deployment/claude-app-pr-42   8080:8080 &

# Tail sandbox logs filtered to one PR
kubectl logs -n pr-42 -l app=claude-app -f

# Run a one-off debug command inside the sandbox container
kubectl exec -n pr-42 deployment/claude-app-pr-42 --   python3 -c "from src.config import validate; validate(); print('Config OK')"

# Check which image is actually running in the sandbox
kubectl get deployment -n pr-42 claude-app-pr-42   -o jsonpath="{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}"
Because the sandbox has its own namespace and no shared state, engineers can run arbitrary debug commands, restart the deployment, or even inject test data without affecting colleagues.
Integrating Sandboxes with Pull Request Reviews
The most useful workflow is to link sandbox smoke-test results directly to the PR status check so reviewers see pass/fail before approving:

# .github/workflows/sandbox.yml — post status check
      - name: Post sandbox smoke result as status check
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
        run: |
          if [ "$SMOKE_EXIT" -eq 0 ]; then
            STATE="success"
            DESC="Sandbox smoke: all cases passed"
          else
            STATE="failure"
            DESC="Sandbox smoke: some cases failed — see CI log"
          fi
          gh api             --method POST             /repos/${{ github.repository }}/statuses/${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}             -f state="$STATE"             -f description="$DESC"             -f context="sandbox/smoke-evals"
With this in place, reviewers see a green or red 'sandbox/smoke-evals' check alongside the standard CI checks before merging.
Summary
Per-PR sandbox environments isolate each branch in its own Kubernetes namespace, eliminating shared-state contention between developers and between CI runs
Sandboxes are provisioned on PR open and destroyed on PR close; a nightly stale sweep handles orphaned namespaces from PRs closed without triggering the workflow
Each sandbox uses an isolated API key (or a shared sandbox-pool key) separate from dev, staging, and production to prevent cross-PR rate-limit contention
Sandbox smoke tests run ≤ 20 eval cases against the sandbox endpoint; full regression evals run only in staging as part of the Gate 3 promotion check
Sandboxes are for pre-review isolation, not for load testing or full red-team exercises — those require the stable 2-replica staging environment
Cost controls: small smoke suites, low rate-limit on sandbox key, and idle scale-down to 0 replicas keep per-PR sandbox cost manageable
